title: An Application of the Universality Theorem for Tverberg Partitions
speaker: Imre Barany
affiliation: Renyi Institute, Hungary and UCL, London
abstract: We show that, as a consequence of a remarkable new result of Attila P\'or on universal Tverberg partitions, any large-enough set $P$ of points in $\Re^d$ has a $(d+2)$-sized subset whose Radon point has half-space depth at least $c_d \cdot |P|$, where $c_d \in (0, 1)$ depends only on $d$. We then give an application of this result to computing weak $\eps$-nets by random sampling. Joint work with Nabil Mustafa.

proof_stages.label: Weak ε-net motivation and segment-hitting target
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines weak ε-nets for convex hulls, recalls known bounds and lower obstructions, and states a motivating segment-hitting variant for large subsets.

proof_stages.label: Radon points and the d+1,d+2 question
proof_stages.summary: Radon partitions and Radon points are introduced, leading to the question whether every sufficiently large point set contains disjoint A and B with |A|=d+2, |B|=d+1, and Radon(A) lying in conv(B).

proof_stages.label: Ramsey-style universality for relations on sequences
proof_stages.summary: A universal, or unavoidable, property is defined as one forced on all k-tuples of a large subsequence, with the framework extended from unordered point sets to ordered sequences and to properties obtained after linear changes of coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Orientation and interlacing Radon universality
proof_stages.summary: Determinant-sign patterns and the cyclic-polytope/moment-curve model are used to explain universal orientation behavior and to identify the interlacing Radon partition as the universal Radon-partition pattern.

proof_stages.label: Universal Tverberg partition problem
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Tverberg’s theorem and its sharp point count, the talk asks which ordered Tverberg partition types, or sets of types, must occur in every sufficiently long point sequence.

proof_stages.label: Special partitions and Pór’s universality theorem
proof_stages.summary: Overlapping blocks in the ordered Tverberg number are defined, special partitions are characterized by meeting every block in every color, and Pór’s theorem is stated: exactly these special partitions are the universal Tverberg partitions.

proof_stages.label: Application to deep Radon points and weak-net consequences
proof_stages.summary: A suitable special coloring forces the d+1,d+2 Radon-containment conclusion, and the argument is strengthened to a positive-fraction theorem producing a Radon point contained in many, equivalently many disjoint, simplices of the point set.

examples.name: Stripe lower-bound construction for weak nets
examples.note: Slicing the point set into stripes each containing about ε|P| points illustrates why any weak ε-net for convex sets needs at least on the order of 1/ε points.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the stripe construction to derive the weak-net lower bound, arguing that slicing into epsilon-sized stripes forces one hitting point per stripe

passes.reason: The parallel-stripe arrangement is a lower-bound enemy configuration forcing one net point per epsilon-heavy stripe.
parallel stripe construction is an extremal lower-bound obstruction forcing one weak-net point per stripe
Parallel stripes form an adversarial lower-bound instance forcing one weak-net point per stripe.
parallel εn-point stripes form an extremal obstruction forcing one weak-net point per stripe
The stripe arrangement is an adversarial lower-bound construction forcing one weak-net point per heavy stripe.
Equal-size parallel stripes are a threshold lower-bound configuration forcing any weak net to use one hit per stripe.
It is used as a lower-bound obstruction to smaller weak nets, and the stripe arrangement is an extremal construction forcing about 1/epsilon hits.
It is used as a lower-bound obstruction forcing one net point per stripe, and the stripe arrangement is an extremal lower-bound configuration.
the stripe arrangement is used as a lower-bound obstruction forcing one weak-net point per stripe, hence a worst-case construction
parallel stripes force one weak-net point per stripe, giving the 1/epsilon lower-bound obstruction
adversarial parallel strips force one weak-net point in each strip, giving the basic lower-bound obstruction.
used as an adversarial lower-bound configuration forcing one net point per disjoint stripe.

examples.name: Planar Radon point configurations
examples.note: In the plane, four points either form a quadrilateral with crossing diagonals or a triangle with an interior point, making the Radon partition definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the two planar four-point Radon configurations, identifying the quadrilateral and triangle-with-interior-point partitions

passes.reason: The quadrilateral and triangle-with-interior-point cases are ordinary planar Radon configurations used to define the Radon point.

four planar points are the minimal Radon circuits used to define the Radon point

examples.name: Seven planar points split into a triangle and two segments
examples.note: The d=2, r=3 Tverberg situation is used to show concretely how a common intersection can arise from one triangle and two segments.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the planar Tverberg case to seven points and reasons that the partition must consist of one triangle and two segments

passes.reason: Seven points give the sharp minimal Tverberg-number case for r=3,d=2, with the partition forced into one triangle and two segments.
seven points is the exact Tverberg number for r=3,d=2, forcing the triangle-and-two-segments type

The seven-point triangle-plus-two-segments partition is a positive Tverberg instance at the tight planar r=3 Tverberg number.
Seven points are exactly the Tverberg number for r=3,d=2, so the triangle-and-two-segments split is a minimal Tverberg configuration.

examples.name: Erdős–Szekeres convex-position theorem
examples.note: The property that every four-tuple is in convex position is presented as a standard universal property for planar point sets.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the Erdős–Szekeres convex-position theorem and its bounds as a universal-property example, without proving or running it on a configuration

passes.reason: Convex-position subsets have all points as hull vertices, the maximal boundary case for planar point sets.
convex-position point sets are extremal by having every point on the convex hull
A convex-position subset maximizes the number of hull vertices, with no point inside the hull of the others.
a convex-position set maximizes the number of hull vertices, and the theorem is cited as a universal-property example
A convex-position set has every point as a hull vertex, the maximal possible hull-vertex configuration.
A convex-position subset has the maximum possible number of hull vertices and serves as a positive universal property.
It illustrates a universal property, while the convex-position subset is extremal among planar point sets by putting every point on the hull.

convex-position subsets have every point on the hull, maximizing the number of extreme points and serving as a positive universal example.

examples.name: Monotone subsequence theorem
examples.note: The one-dimensional three-term order property is used as a simple sequence-based universal property, equivalent to finding a long increasing or decreasing subsequence.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the monotone subsequence theorem via the between-point inequalities and the n^2+1 bound, without deriving it or applying it to a concrete sequence

passes.reason: A monotone subsequence is an inversion-minimum or inversion-maximum order pattern, used as a positive universal example.
a monotone subsequence is an order-extreme pattern with zero or maximal inversions
A monotone subsequence is the maximally ordered one-dimensional pattern used as a positive universal example.
a monotone subsequence has the extremal zero-or-all inversion order pattern, used as a positive universal example
A monotone subsequence is the maximally ordered permutation pattern, with zero or all inversions.
A monotone subsequence is the completely ordered inversion-free extreme order pattern in one dimension.
It is a positive Ramsey-type example, and a monotone subsequence is an extremal ordered pattern with all comparisons aligned.
A monotone subsequence is a maximally ordered extreme pattern among real sequences, used as a positive universal theorem.
the monotone subsequence is the one-dimensional base case with all comparisons aligned in an extreme regular order pattern
a monotone subsequence is the no-inversion or all-inversion extreme order pattern
a monotone subsequence is the maximally ordered zero- or maximum-inversion pattern in the Erdős–Szekeres theorem.
a monotone subsequence is an inversion-free chain, the extreme order pattern in a real sequence.

examples.name: q-spaced subsequence after moving the origin
examples.note: The failure of q-separated ratios for numbers clustered between 100 and 101 motivates allowing an affine change of coordinates before asking for q-geometric growth or decay.
examples.engagement_reason: he works through ratios, the obstruction near 100–101, shifting the origin, reflection, and affine transformation to get q-spaced behavior

passes.reason: After shifting the origin, the subsequence has lacunary multiplicative gaps, a strongly separated boundary-type structure.

examples.name: Higher-dimensional q-geometric vector sequences
examples.note: Ratios of coordinates of vectors are used to display a higher-dimensional Ramsey-type universal property after an invertible linear transformation.
